Hebrews 6:13: “when God made a promise to Abraham, since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself.” The divine self-witness in the triple name is the covenant-oath form that anticipates the Father’s testimony to the Son (John 5:37: “the Father who sent me has himself borne witness about me”).
22.22: A type: if it was in rebellion or in breach of faith against YHWH — do not spare us today. The eastern tribes submit to the western community’s judgment: if we are guilty, execute us. The willingness to accept the full covenant penalty if proven guilty is the form of covenant integrity. Christ’s submission to the Father’s judgment — “not my will, but yours, be done” (Luke 22:42) — is the perfect form of this submission. He who was not guilty submitted to the full penalty on behalf of those who were: the ultimate inversion of the eastern tribes’ conditional submission (they said “punish us if guilty”; Christ said “I will receive the punishment for the guilty”).
22.23: A type: if we built the altar to turn away from following YHWH — let YHWH himself require it. The self-imprecation with YHWH as the executor of the penalty is the covenant-oath’s ultimate form: appealing to YHWH himself as both judge and executioner if they lie. The logic anticipates Hebrews 10:28-31: the severity of covenant judgment under the old covenant was real; how much more severe the judgment for trampling the Son of God underfoot (v29). The eastern tribes’ self-imprecation with YHWH as judge is the shadow of the “it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God” (Heb 10:31).
22.24: A type: the explanation — they built the altar because they feared future generations would be told “what have you to do with YHWH the God of Israel? YHWH has made the Jordan a boundary between us and you.” The fear that geographic separation would become covenantal separation drives the altar’s construction. The anxiety about being cut off from the covenant community by a physical boundary is the pastoral heart of the eastern tribes’ action. Christ’s own prayer (John 17:11: “Holy Father, keep them in your name that you have given me, that they may be one, even as we are one”) addresses the same fear: that the physical separation of death and distance might break the unity of his community.
22.25: A type: “YHWH has made the Jordan a boundary between us and you, you people of Reuben and Gad; you have no portion in YHWH” — the feared future exclusion. The anxiety that the Jordan’s boundary would create a theological second-class — people with “no portion in YHWH” — is the covenant-exclusion fear that the altar addresses. In the NT, the dividing wall between Jew and Gentile created exactly this anxiety (Eph 2:12: Gentiles were “alienated from the commonwealth of Israel and strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world”). Christ’s reconciling work eliminates the dividing boundary: no Jordan-boundary, no Gentile-exclusion, in the one body.
22.26: A type: “Therefore we said, ‘Let us now build an altar’ — not for burnt offering nor for sacrifice, but to be a witness between us and you.” The witness-altar is a monument to covenant unity, not a rival place of sacrifice. The distinction between the sacrificial altar (where YHWH’s atonement is enacted) and the witness monument (that testifies to covenant belonging) is critical. There is one altar of sacrifice: Christ’s cross (Heb 10:10-14: “by that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all”). All subsequent monuments and memorials — including the Lord’s Supper — are witness-monuments to the one sacrifice, not supplements to it.
22.27: A type: the altar as witness that they perform the service of YHWH before him with burnt offerings and sacrifices — lest future generations say they have no portion. The witness function of the altar is explicitly forward-looking: it is for future generations, not present benefit. The Lord’s Supper is the NT counterpart — “do this in remembrance of me... until he comes” (1 Cor 11:24-26) — a forward-looking witness monument that declares the covenant meal’s validity for every future generation until Christ returns. The Jordan altar anticipates the Supper’s trans-generational witness function.
22.28: A type: “if they say this to us in the future, we shall reply ‘Behold the copy of the altar of YHWH which our fathers made.’” The altar as the physical argument against future exclusion — a copy (tabnit) of the true altar, a witness monument — is the type of every covenant memorial that grounds present identity in past act. Baptism functions similarly in the NT: the water memorial declares the one baptism into Christ’s death and resurrection (Rom 6:3-4) as the ground of present covenant identity. The copy-altar points back to the original act; baptism points back to the defining act of death and resurrection.
22.29: A type: “Far be it from us (chalilah lanu) that we should rebel against YHWH and turn away this day from following YHWH by building an altar for burnt offerings, grain offerings, or sacrifices, other than the altar of YHWH our God that stands before his tabernacle.” The eastern tribes’ strongest possible rejection of any rival sacrifice-altar affirms the one sanctuary principle. There is one altar of atonement: the tabernacle/temple altar, to which all legitimate sacrifice belongs. In the NT, there is one atonement: the cross of Christ. Hebrews 13:10: “we have an altar from which those who serve the tent have no right to eat” — Christ’s sacrifice is the one altar that the entire new covenant community shares.
22.30: A type: when Phinehas and the chiefs of the congregation heard the words of the Reubenites and Gadites and Manassites, it was good in their eyes. The resolution — the confrontation leads to understanding, which produces peace — is the form of Matthew 18:15: “if he listens to you, you have gained your brother.” The process of direct confrontation, full explanation, and public vindication is the covenant community’s conflict-resolution mechanism working as YHWH designed it. The near-civil-war ends not with conquest but with comprehension — a more complete victory than military force could achieve.
22.31: A type: Phinehas said, “Today we know that YHWH is in our midst, because you have not committed this breach of faith against YHWH. Now you have delivered the people of Israel from the hand of YHWH.” The priest’s declaration — YHWH is in our midst because you acted faithfully — makes covenant integrity the condition of divine presence. The obverse is equally true: covenant faithfulness preserves divine presence, while ma'al drives it away. This principle reaches its fulfillment in Christ’s promise: “where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them” (Matt 18:20) — the community’s faithful covenant practice becomes the locus of divine presence.
22.32: A type: Phinehas and the chiefs returned from the land of Gilead to the land of Canaan and reported to the people of Israel. The resolution’s public announcement — the ambassador’s return and report — completes the covenant process: confrontation, investigation, resolution, public reporting. The report is the form of 2 Corinthians 5:20 (“we are ambassadors for Christ, God making his appeal through us”) — the ambassador who goes, investigates, reconciles, and returns with news of peace. Phinehas’s embassy is one of the OT’s clearest models of the gospel-ambassador role.
22.33: A type: the people of Israel were pleased, and blessed God and spoke no more of making war against them, and did not go up to destroy the land. The averted civil war — the near-catastrophe resolved by faithful process — leaves the community at peace. The blessing of God that the community offers after the resolution is the covenantal doxology: praise is the right response to preserved unity. Romans 15:5-6: “May the God of endurance and encouragement grant you to live in such harmony with one another... so that together you may with one voice glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ.”
22.34: A type: the Reubenites and Gadites called the altar Witness (Ed), “for it is a witness between us that YHWH is God.” The altar named “Witness” becomes a permanent physical declaration: YHWH is God, and this stone testifies to the covenant unity of the separated communities. Revelation 3:14 names Christ as “the faithful and true witness (martus)” — the ultimate Witness who testifies that YHWH is God and that his covenant people are one. The Jordan altar’s name is a type: the true Ed/Witness between God and his people, between the separated communities of east and west, Jew and Gentile, is Christ himself.

23.1: A type: after a long time — when YHWH had given Israel rest from all their surrounding enemies — Joshua summoned all Israel. The rest-gift that enables Joshua’s farewell is the Canaan-rest that Hebrews identifies as the provisional fulfillment pointing to the eschatological rest. Hebrews 4:8: “if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day later on.” The rest of Canaan (real, historical, comprehensive — YHWH gave it) is not the final rest but the pattern and pledge of it. The “long time afterward” before Joshua’s farewell is the fullness of the land-rest period; the “another day” of Psalm 95 and Hebrews 4 is the fullness of the Christ-rest that remains.
23.2: A type: Joshua summoned all Israel — its elders, leaders, judges, and officers. The full covenant leadership assembly for Joshua’s farewell mirrors the full assembly at Sinai and the Shechem renewal (ch. 24). The death-discourse of the covenant leader — the farewell speech that becomes the covenant community’s inheritance — is the form of Moses’s Deuteronomy farewell and Christ’s upper-room discourse (John 13-17). The dying leader’s last words are not biography but covenant theology: what Israel needs to know to survive and thrive in the land is what Joshua teaches in his dying speech.
23.3: A type: “You yourselves have seen all that YHWH your God has done to all these nations for your sake, for it is YHWH your God who has fought for you.” Joshua’s summary of the conquest as YHWH fighting for Israel is the theological verdict that the military narrative embodies. Romans 8:31: “If God is for us, who can be against us?” is the NT form of this summary: the rhetorical question assumes the answer of Joshua 23:3 as its premise. Paul’s confidence in God’s advocacy is grounded in the conquest history — the pattern of YHWH fighting for his people has its ultimate expression in God’s not sparing his own Son (Rom 8:32).
23.4: A type: Joshua allotted to Israel the remaining nations as an inheritance for the tribes, from the Jordan to the Great Sea in the west. The inheritance of what is not yet possessed — the nations allotted as future inheritance even while some remain unconquered — is the form of the “already/not yet” eschatological inheritance. Hebrews 11:1: “faith is the assurance of things hoped for, the conviction of things not seen.” The tribes receive their allotment as inheritance even before full possession — faith takes what the divine grant gives before experiential possession is complete.
23.5: A direct revelation (through Joshua): “YHWH your God will push them back before you and drive them out of your sight, and you shall possess their land, just as YHWH your God promised you.” The future-tense divine promise for the remaining conquest is the same form as Philippians 1:6: “he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Jesus Christ.” Joshua’s farewell does not minimize the remaining work but grounds the expectation of completion in the same YHWH who has already accomplished so much. The promise of completing what was begun is the structure of Christian hope.
23.6: A direct revelation: “Therefore be very strong to keep and to do all that is written in the Book of the Law of Moses, turning aside from it neither to the right hand nor to the left.” The strength-for-obedience formula (cf. Josh 1:7: “be strong and very courageous, being careful to do according to all the law”) frames the covenant obligation as requiring active, sustained commitment to the written Torah. Matthew 5:17-18: “Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them... not an iota, not a dot, will pass from the Law until all is accomplished.” The total Torah-fidelity Joshua commands is answered in Christ’s perfect fulfillment — the completion that Joshua could command but not achieve.
23.7: A type: “that you may not mix with these nations remaining among you or make mention of the names of their gods or swear by them or serve them or bow down to them.” The four-fold prohibition against the remaining nations’ religious influence — mention, oath, service, worship — covers every level of religious engagement from speech to body. Second Corinthians 6:14: “do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers” — Paul’s application of the separation principle to the church extends Joshua’s covenantal warning. The warning against being shaped by the surrounding culture’s gods is perennial: every generation of the covenant community faces the same temptation to accommodate the idol-systems of its neighbors.
23.8: A type: “But you shall cling to YHWH your God just as you have done to this day.” The verb dabaq (cling/cleave) is the same word used in Genesis 2:24 (“a man shall leave his father and his mother and hold fast to his wife”) and Ruth 1:14 (Ruth “clung to” Naomi). The covenantal clinging is the marriage-fidelity metaphor: total attachment, irreversible loyalty. Romans 8:38-39: “neither death nor life... will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord” — the NT fulfillment is not Israel’s clinging to YHWH (which will fail, as the subsequent history shows) but Christ’s inseparable love clinging to his own. The dabaq becomes unconditional when Christ does the holding.
23.9: A type: YHWH has driven out before you great and strong nations, and no man has been able to stand against you to this day. The invincibility of Israel when YHWH fights for them is the historical testimony that grounds the covenant exhortation. The invincibility principle is not military but covenantal: Israel’s strength is entirely derivative of YHWH’s fighting-for. In Christ, the same pattern: “we are more than conquerors through him who loved us” (Rom 8:37) — the conquest is through the one who fights for us, not through our own military or spiritual capability.
23.10: A type: “One man of you puts to flight a thousand, since it is YHWH your God who fights for you, just as he promised.” The arithmetic of divine empowerment — 1,000:1 — is the holy-war ratio that signals divine presence: normal military math is reversed when the divine warrior is the true combatant. Philippians 4:13: “I can do all things through him who strengthens me.” The one-against-a-thousand effectiveness is not the warrior’s skill but the divine presence’s empowerment. Paul’s “all things through him” is the NT form of the holy-war ratio: the believer’s disproportionate effectiveness comes entirely from the indwelling divine power.
23.11: A direct revelation: “Be very careful, therefore, to love YHWH your God.” The command to love YHWH — stated as the single most important exhortation in Joshua’s farewell — is the Shema’s heart distilled. First John 4:19: “we love because he first loved us” is the NT answer to Joshua’s command: the love that Joshua urgently commands is made possible by the prior love of Christ. The “be very careful to love” shows that love is not merely a feeling but a disciplined covenant commitment — and the ground of that disciplined love is the prior unconditional love of the one who commands it.
23.12: A type: if you turn back and cling to the remnant of these nations remaining among you and make marriages with them — the warning against covenant-mixing. The verb dabaq (“cling”) appears again but now negatively: the wrong clinging is to the nations instead of to YHWH. The ironic inversion is stark: the command of verse 8 is to cling to YHWH; the warning of verse 12 is against clinging to the nations. Second Corinthians 6:14: “do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers.” The clinging that belongs to YHWH being redirected to the nations is the fundamental covenant apostasy — and its practical form is intermarriage and social integration with those who serve other gods.
23.13: A type: the remaining nations will become “a snare and a trap for you, a whip on your sides and thorns in your eyes, until you perish from this good land.” The four metaphors of covenant curse from the remaining nations — snare, trap, whip, thorns — are the instruments of the judgment that the subsequent history will enact. First Corinthians 15:33: “do not be deceived: bad company corrupts good morals.” The same principle — proximity to false worship and practice corrupts covenant integrity — is Paul’s application to the Corinthian church. The thorns-in-eyes image echoes the wilderness experience (Num 33:55: “those you allow to remain will become barbs in your eyes and thorns in your sides”) — the same warning reiterated at the land’s reception.
23.14: A direct revelation (Joshua’s personal testimony): “I am about to go the way of all the earth, and you know in your hearts and souls, all of you, that not one word has failed of all the good things that YHWH your God promised concerning you; all have come to pass.” This is the OT’s most comprehensive single statement of divine promise-faithfulness: not one word failed. Second Corinthians 1:20: “For all the promises of God find their Yes in him [Christ].” Joshua’s territorial testimony (all land-promises fulfilled) is the type; Paul’s christological testimony (all covenant promises fulfilled in Christ) is the antitype. The lo nafal davar (not one word fell/failed) of Joshua’s dying testimony becomes the Yes and Amen of the gospel.
23.15: A type: just as all the good things that YHWH promised have come to pass for you, so YHWH will bring upon you all the evil things until he has destroyed you from off this good land. The double-edged faithfulness of YHWH — the same reliability that guarantees blessings guarantees curses — is Joshua’s most sobering teaching. Hebrews 10:28-29: “anyone who has set aside the law of Moses dies without mercy on the evidence of two or three witnesses. How much worse punishment, do you think, will be deserved by the one who has trampled underfoot the Son of God?” The principle of covenant symmetry — equal reliability of blessing and curse — is intensified in the new covenant: if YHWH’s land-curses were real and swift, the new covenant consequences for rejecting Christ are proportionally more severe.
23.16: A type: “if you transgress the covenant of YHWH your God, which he commanded you, and go and serve other gods and bow down to them. Then the anger of YHWH will be kindled against you, and you shall perish quickly from off the good land that he has given to you.” The final covenant warning is simple and direct: the path from idolatry to exile is short. Revelation 20:9: the final gathering of all who have aligned against God ends with fire from heaven consuming them — the eschatological form of the covenant curse Joshua warns about. The temporal land-loss Israel will experience from covenant violation is the historical enactment of a principle that has its ultimate expression in the final judgment, where those who serve other gods are excluded from the new creation.
